session.title: When the Shadow Crosses Over
session.description:
This session provides a technical overview of Remote Desktop shadowing in Windows environments. We will begin by reviewing prior research on how shadowing is enabled and used via Microsoft-provided binaries. We will then take a broader look at Remote Assistance to understand how it differs from shadowing.

Finally, we will explore how the same functionality can now be achieved from a Linux or macOS system using existing open-source projects—simplifying testing workflows by removing the need for a dedicated Windows VM, intermediate access paths, or additional tooling.

session.title: Using Volatility 3 to Detect Sophisticated Malware
session.description: Volatility 3 is the latest version of the Volatility Memory Analysis framework, which has been the most widely used open-source framework for memory forensics since its creation in 2007. This new version of the framework is a complete rewrite starting from the first line of code. In this presentation, attendees will learn about Volatility 3’s new features while also seeing how many brand-new plugins can be used to detect a wide range of sophisticated, modern malware. This will include detection of the techniques currently deployed by ransomware and APT groups to evade EDR detection, inject code in a stealthy manner, and perform lateral movement. Attendees will leave understanding how to detect modern malware and attacker toolkits along with how to integrate Volatility 3 and its new features into detection workflows suitable for production use.

session.title: Living off the (land)cloud: Scattered Spider and the cloud control plane
session.description:
Scattered Spider has escalated its exploits in 2025, expanding beyond telcos to target enterprises across industry verticals. After gaining initial access through social engineering, the threat group is increasingly targeting cloud workloads, to elevate permissions for lateral movement. Attacks targeting the cloud control plane, focused on the IAM service, provide the attacker access to high-value assets and sensitive data. These attacks, known as Living off the Cloud attacks, avoid malware and leverage built-in cloud primitives for offensive activity.

This session focuses on identity exploits as part of the overall attack lifecycle of Scattered Spider. Through real-world attack demonstrations and analysis, the session outlines the stealthy enumeration tactics to identify privileged identities, IAM-specific exploits to elevate permissions, and lateral movement. 

The session covers the defender’s perspective, what makes detection challenging as attackers pivot across control and data planes, and provides a practitioner’s learnings on countermeasures to defend against cloud identity exploits.

session.title: Trust at Scale: Lessons Learned from a Decade of Engineering for Identity
session.description:
What does it take to build a security product that millions trust with their most sensitive information? Over the past decade, I have worked as the CTO at Dashlane, on a journey from a consumer password manager to a B2B identity security platform. It has been rich with breakthroughs—and equally rich with mistakes. In this talk, I will share stories from the trenches: the architectural decisions that shaped (and sometimes constrained) the product, the unseen costs of early technical debt, the real-world impact of evolving encryption standards, and how user expectations forced innovation in usability without compromising security.
I will map key technical milestones to business and product shifts, illustrating how scaling trust isn't just about technology—it's about culture, trade-offs, and relentless adaptation.
Attendees will walk away with practical insights into building secure-by-design systems, navigating B2C and B2B pivots, and leading engineering and security teams through constant change in the unforgiving identity security space.

session.title: Atomic Red Team 101
session.description:
Red Canary will be presenting a 2-hour workshop on the Atomic Red Team open-source project. This workshop will explore how Atomic Red Team testing can be used to emulate threats, validate security controls and improve overall security. Attendees will go hands on with Atomic Red Team, using the Invoke-Atomic test harness, crafting emulation plans and working through incorporating regular atomic testing in their own environment.

The Atomic Red Team 101 Workshop will focus on the following: 

- Overview of Atomic Red Team (ART)
- MITRE ATT&CK Framework and Threat Emulation
- Configuring a Windows testing environment
- Atomic Red Team and Invoke-Atomic installation
- Crafting a Ransomware Threat Emulation plan
- Detection and Response Validation

Attendees will be given a cloud-based lab environment in which to work through the extensive hands-on portion along with a lab manual and slide deck for future reference.

session.title: Trust the Model? Weaponizing Windows AI for Payload Staging
session.description:
This talk explores how attackers can stage and execute payloads using Microsoft’s native AI stack specifically the Windows.AI.MachineLearning (WinML) API built into Windows 10/11. By embedding data inside ONNX model metadata or tensor fields, an attacker can deliver payloads through models that appear benign to both users and security tools.

I will demonstrate how to abuse LearningModel::LoadFromFilePath() and model.Metadata() to extract and run payloads from simple file creation to full in-memory shellcode execution using only Microsoft-signed AI components and standard Windows APIs like CreateFileW and VirtualAlloc.

The payload travels inside a model and inference itself becomes the loader. I will present a working PoC, discuss where ML pipelines intersect with trusted execution, and explore how defenders can detect and mitigate AI-layer abuse.

Attendees will leave with new insight into abusing and defending ML surfaces on Windows, including tooling, TTPs, and what to log when AI models become malicious.

session.title: Building a security startup as an outsider
session.description:
In an industry dominated by security veterans and technical specialists, Leen (our startup) took an unconventional path. This talk reveals how two non-security professionals joined forces with one security expert to create a company that's challenging industry norms. We'll share how our backgrounds in food delivery, consumer/B2B SaaS, and venture capital equipped us with fresh perspectives on persistent security challenges.

Learn how we've applied lessons from consumer-focused industries to security, prioritizing design thinking, compelling marketing narratives, and content-driven growth strategies that traditional security vendors often overlook. We'll discuss how our 'outsider' status became our greatest advantage, enabling us to question established approaches and build solutions that resonate with both technical and non-technical stakeholders.

This session offers practical insights for aspiring founders without traditional security credentials, established security professionals seeking fresh GTM approaches, and anyone interested in how cross-industry experience can drive innovation in specialized technical fields.

session.title: From pocket to Pwn: How we hacked a multinational corp for $200 with whats in our pockets
session.description:
Not all Red Team engagements are spearphish > mimikatz > profit. If nothing else, this talk proves that some jobs still involve crawling around car parks at night and hiding in bushes with a high gain antenna to gain your objective.

Traditionally, cyber incidents and Red Team operations focus on compromising Windows devices as an initial infection vector but for modern cloud native companies, this approach simply doesn’t work.

In this talk, we will explore how we compromised an Electric Vehicle to infiltrate a corporate Smartphone, leveraging legitimate applications and protocols to move laterally and achieve full compromise of the environment.

We will demonstrate the chaining of low code (and in some cases ancient techniques), abstract attack vectors targeting atypical devices and platforms and the abuse of protocols and inbuilt applications to conduct a full scale breach of the environment.

session.title: The Log Rings Don’t Lie: Historical Enumeration in Plain Sight
session.description:
Logs keep a lot of data. Most of them are events, but events have other data in them. Source IP, Source Identity, Target Resource, User Agent, Request and Response Parameters, etc. Which one of them are important? All of them of course. Logs are important to a defender. And they are a needle in the foot of the attacker. But why do they have to be? Why can't the attacker actually profit from logs? What if the attacker can achieve enumeration, C2 capabilities, exfiltration, download through logs? 
This presentation will go through actually that. How can an attacker utilize Logs to attack a Cloud Infrastructure.

session.title: The History of Malware: From Floppies to Droppers
session.description: Modern malware, such as ransomware, has become synonymous with some of the most devastating cyber attacks of our time.. But it hasn't always been so. Not too long ago, malware was considered a myth. The first ransomware, for example, was created over 30 years ago as a wild scheme, devised by a man armed with 10,000 floppy disks and a virus. Since then, malware has evolved in many different ways, as technology changes and evolves. Looking back and analyzing this history gives us an unusual perspective- what elements of malware have changed throughout the years, and what has remained consistent? How has this evolved into the most impactful form of cybercrime today, and what can this surprising, untold history teach us about our present and future?

session.title: When Build vs Buy Is Rigged: Selling to Enterprises That Prefer Building In-House
session.description: In security, the real competition isn’t other vendors. It’s their in-house script written by a staff engineer who left the company in 2018. Some security teams would spend 6 months building a brittle, undocumented internal tool just to avoid 3 weeks time in procurement. It is a trust, ego, and culture problem. In this talk, I’ll share hard-earned lessons as a repeat founder, navigating procurement barriers in FinTech, HealthTech, and media companies. We’ll unpack the psychology of in-house builders, how to earn trust without selling FUD, and how to become the tool they wish they had built.

session.title: Inside Ransomware: Facts and Findings from the Blackbasta and Lockbit Leaks
session.description:
When an anonymous actor calling themselves “ExploitWhispers” posted nearly a year’s worth of BlackBasta’s internal Matrix chats in February 2025, the industry received an unfiltered window into the criminal enterprise behind dozens of high-profile intrusions.

Twelve weeks later, a separate breach dumped the entire MySQL backend of LockBit’s affiliate panel. 20 tables covering build pipelines, negotiation transcripts, and cryptocurrency payout data were released onto public code-sharing sites with a Tor-site defacement confirming the compromise. Together these disclosures offer something incident responders rarely get: the attackers own words, workflows, and source artifacts.

In this talk we will take a deep dive into those data sets—walking through the process of parsing thousands of lines of attacker conversations, configuration files, and build logs to surface the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) that drive day-to-day ransomware operations.

This talk will focus on the approach for processing these large data sets, how affiliate recruitment, initial access, payload testing, negotiation, and cash-out weave together into a repeatable playbook, and the CI/CD build pipelines which allow for rapid development and deployment of malicious payloads.

Attendees will leave with a comprehensive understanding of how ransomware crews operate, insights into their financial gains, and the underlying motivations that drive their activities.

session.title: P0LR Espresso - Pulling Shots of Cloud Live Response & Advanced Analysis
session.description:
In today’s detection landscape, defenders are overwhelmed by dashboards and visualizations that are often aesthetically pleasing but lack actionable insight. These tools rarely answer the core question: Is this identity compromised?

P0LR Espresso, from name itself, lets you understand, that this tool is meant to be cold and direct. Like Espresso (Itl. Esprimere) ~ Press Out/Squeeze, P0LR Espresso, presses out and squeezes threats from a bunch of noisy logs across multiple cloud integrations (PaaS, IdP and SaaS) and brings them in front of our eyes using meaningful and clear visualizations! This tool has multi-cloud integration capabilities, normalizes field names, saving defender’s time trying to understand each integration’s structure, together with Live Response and Detection capabilities, which also makes it possible to create detections in real time and scan your logs in search for evil.

P0LR Espresso empowers threat hunters, detection engineers, and incident responders to rapidly uncover an identity’s activity timeline, detect behavioral shifts, and pinpoint suspicious activity. With built-in detection capabilities, it’s especially effective when rapidly scanning normalized logs for known IOCs, triggering alerts on suspicious patterns, and jumpstarting investigations in high-pressure scenarios.

Come pull up a chair and let us pull you a shot of P0LR Espresso - putting the "express" in Live Response for combatting cloud-focused threats.

session.title: The Allure of Go's Cross-Platform Capability: A Gateway for Threat Actors to Mac and Linux
session.description:
Go-Based Malware Targeting Mac and Linux Systems
This paper examines how malware authors leverage Go's cross-platform capabilities to target Mac and Linux systems, exploring unique attributes, detection challenges, and hunting strategies for this growing threat vector.

Key Focus Areas:
Cross-Platform Variations: Go malware optimized for Mac and Linux shows distinct attributes across families. Examples include DragonFly APT's backdoors and BlackCat/ALPHV's ransomware implementations.

Detection Challenges: Go's compilation characteristics create substantial detection hurdles on non-Windows platforms, with a 43% increase in detection difficulty versus traditional malware.

Reverse Engineering: Analysis of Go-based malware requires specialized approaches to overcome Go-specific obfuscation. Groups like Lazarus and APT29 increasingly use Go for its resistance to reverse engineering.

Hunting Strategies: Effective identification requires specialized approaches leveraging threat intelligence and IoCs. Go-based malware targeting Mac/Linux has increased 267% in 18 months.

Future Trends: Go-based malware now represents 11.8% of all new malware (up from 4.3% in 2021). Sophisticated actors including TeamTNT, SideWinder APT, and ransomware operations increasingly adopt Go to expand beyond Windows environments.

This research highlights an emerging threat exploiting Go's advantages to target previously underrepresented platforms in the malware landscape.

session.title: Rebooting the cyber arsenal of democracy
session.description:
Starting a cybersecurity company has never been easier - and yet, never more brutally competitive. With the explosion of AI tools, the collapse of traditional GTM tactics, and VC capital tightening across the board, today’s founder must be faster, leaner, and more resilient than ever.

Eric Foster has been part of building and exiting multiple successful security companies, including RiskIQ (acquired by Microsoft) and Cyderes (acquired by private equity). Today, he’s building TENEX.AI - an AI security company backed by Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) - and he’s doing it in the crucible of rising threats, shifting platform power, and a rapidly evolving venture market.

In this no-BS, field-tested talk, Eric shares what he’s learned across his arc: what it really takes to start a security company in 2025, how to raise venture capital from the right partners (and avoid the wrong ones), how to and not to be successful at the most challenging aspect of starting a company (GTM) - and why the next generation of cybersecurity leadership has a moral imperative to rebuild the digital arsenal of democracy. This isn’t theory. It’s war stories, scar tissue, and tactical playbooks - straight from the front lines.

Founders, operators, and future entrepreneurs: if you’re thinking about building in security, this is the session you can’t afford to miss.

session.title: Interactive Networking Fingerprinting Walktrough
session.description:
The goal of this interactive workshop is to equip participants with a hands‑on understanding of modern passive network fingerprinting techniques - JA3, JA3N, JA4, p0f and MuonFP - and the ability to generate, interpret and apply these fingerprints in real‑world defensive workflows.

Key Takeaways:
Understanding the Evolution of Fingerprinting: Why we moved from p0f to MuonFP and from JA3 to JA4.
Hands-on capturing fingerprints: generating JA3/JA4/p0f/MuonFP fingerprints.
Understanding the limitations of fingerprints.

session.title: Inside Cloud Attack Paths: End-to-End Adversary Simulation
session.description: Adversaries map and navigate cloud attack paths by chaining misconfigurations, over-privileged principals, and exposed secrets in pursuit of privilege escalation and broader objectives. In this talk, we explore core attack primitives in Azure, Entra ID, and Microsoft 365, demonstrate how to simulate end-to-end attack chains, and analyze the resulting telemetry to uncover the traces these attacks leave behind.

session.title: Sniffing Out Cert Abuse: A Dogged Approach to ESC Remediation
session.description:
Active Directory Certificate Services (ADCS) have become a critical yet often overlooked attack vector in enterprise environments. Following the high-profile "Certified Pre-Owned" research, organizations are increasingly seeking pragmatic approaches to remediate certificate-based privilege escalation vulnerabilities (ESC1–ESC8).

This session introduces a structured approach to ADCS security using BloodHound Enterprise. ADCS remediation empowers security teams to identify, prioritize, and remediate ESC paths. Attendees will learn how this methodology integrates attack path analysis, strategic remediation planning, and validation to reduce real-world risk.

session.title: Hidden Payloads: Advanced Malware Exploitation via PDF Files
session.description:
This is a hands-on presentation that will guide you through the world of PDF exploitation, showcasing how this ubiquitous document format can serve as a vessel for malicious JavaScript malware. Dive into real-world vulnerabilities that have been leveraged to execute harmful code directly through PDF files—posing major threats in today's cybersecurity landscape.

Key exploit techniques explored will include:

Heap Spray Attacks: Gain insight into how attackers use shellcode to overwrite memory, enabling arbitrary code execution and sys­tem compromise.
Data Exfiltration Tactics: Discover methods for covertly extracting sensitive data, such as email addresses and system information, from unsuspecting users.
Embedding Malware in PDFs: Learn how adversaries embed malicious scripts within PDF files, tricking users into triggering exploits in Adobe Reader through typical file interactions.
We'll dissect techniques including shellcode injection, buffer overflow attacks, Adobe Reader exploitation, and memory manipulation—each engineered to deliver and execute malware efficiently.

This session is ideal for offensive security professionals, penetration testers, and threat emulation experts seeking to elevate their understanding of PDF-based threats and enhance their testing skills. 

More information about the presentation can be found in this article: https://labs.segura.blog/unmasking-the-threat-a-deep-dive-into-the-pdf-malicious-2/

session.title: Contribute to Learn: Building DFIR Expertise Through Open Source
session.description:
The best way to learn digital forensics and incident response (DFIR) isn’t just by reading books or watching videos—it’s by doing. Contributing to open-source DFIR tools like KAPE and Velociraptor offers a hands-on, real-world way to build, apply, and deepen your technical knowledge.

In this talk, we’ll explore how contributing—whether through writing code, testing artifacts, improving documentation, or validating forensic results—forces you to engage critically with data structures, logs, operating system internals, and investigative workflows. I’ll share practical examples of how even small contributions can lead to big “a-ha” moments, demystify what it’s like to get started in open source, and show how this process naturally reinforces and expands your DFIR skillset.

But the benefits go beyond just technical skill. Open-source work also connects you with a global community of practitioners, tool authors, and potential mentors. Contributing is a powerful way to get your name out there, receive feedback, and become part of the broader DFIR conversation through active participation.

If you're looking to level up your skills, grow your network, and learn by doing, this talk will show you how the path to deeper DFIR knowledge might just start with a pull request.

session.title: Breaking the Mobile Log Analysis Barrier with AI
session.description: Mobile security teams face overwhelming volumes of device logs during incident response, malware investigations, and vulnerability hunting. Traditional manual analysis of these logs can take security experts hours or days per device, creating significant bottlenecks, further compounded by the scarcity of specialized mobile security expertise. A forensic analysis framework leveraging traditional natural language processing approaches, along with large language models, can automatically analyze Android bug reports and Apple iOS unified logs at scale. This approach transforms complex, unstructured technical data into clear, comprehensible insights that reveal the complete security narrative of a device. By automatically connecting seemingly disparate events into coherent timelines, the methodology identifies critical security issues across device logs in minutes rather than days. The result is an immediate democratization of mobile forensics insights–anyone can understand what’s happening on their device, from security professionals to end users with no specialized mobile operating system knowledge. Importantly, all analysis is performed within a secure, controlled environment using privacy-preserving techniques that maintain data confidentiality. Through real-world case studies, this presentation will demonstrate how this approach fundamentally transforms mobile security operations by revealing hidden threats while making complex technical findings universally accessible.

session.title: Why Being A Great Technologist May Not Make You A Great CEO (And How To Bridge The Gap)
session.description:
Technical excellence and business leadership don’t always optimize for the same outcomes. While engineers and software architects often seek elegant solutions and innovation, CEOs are prioritizing customers' needs and time-to-market. Enterprise buyers are often willing to accept an imperfect solution, as long as it works and it’s available right now.

In this talk, SixMap CEO Jason Kaplan will discuss why great technologists don’t automatically make great CEOs. Technologists can of course become outstanding CEOs but, like anything else in life, becoming a great business leader requires time, effort, and lots of learning (usually the hard way).

Jason will share some of the lessons he’s learned along the way of his 27 year career to help technologists mature into more effective CEOs. The focus will be on the areas that technologists often find most challenging: building teams and managing people, communicating to executives, making fast decisions with lots of uncertainty, and balancing the tradeoffs between product development and time-to-market. 

Ultimately, success in cybersecurity entrepreneurship isn't about having the perfect plan—it's about surviving the chaos long enough to figure out a plan that works.

session.title: Slaying Hidden Threats in Residential (and Mobile!) IP Proxies
session.description: Residential proxies are the weapon of choice for bot bypassing defenses by mimicking legit traffic. This talk unpacks how machine learning can expose and mitigate these threats at scale. Expect actionable insights for improving detections while minimizing false positives.

session.title: Beyond Vibe Coding: Building Reliable AI AppSec Tools
session.description:
As organizations explore AI automation for AppSec, ensuring reliable and trustworthy output becomes critical. This talk examines practical challenges in building AI systems that can consistently interpret security requirements, process engineering documentation, and produce high quality threat models and code scanning results. 

We'll explore technical approaches to prevent hallucinations, handle conflicting documentation, normalize AI outputs, and validate assessments against established policies. Drawing from real-world implementation experience, we'll share key patterns for building robust security automation systems that maintain high accuracy while scaling across engineering organizations.

session.title: From CISA to Starting Up: Shifting Secure by Design at Scale
session.description:
How do you shift an entire industry? This talk explores my journey from leading the Secure by Design initiative at CISA to founding Corridor, an AI assistant for product security teams. This work stems from the core realization that most cyberattacks are preventable—not because we need better defenders, but because software is still shipped insecure by default. Software companies have the ability to prevent entire classes of vulnerabilities, and addressing this is equal parts tech and policy.

I’ll share lessons from the policy side – starting the Secure by Design pledge, writing legislation, and shaping executive orders; the business side – the process of starting a startup, fundraising, and finding product-market fit; and the technical side – how we use AI to understand entire codebases and aid product security teams with otherwise-manual workflows.

session.title: Navigating the Virtualization Battlefield: A Deep Dive into Hypervisor Attack Vectors
session.description:
The intricate architecture of hypervisors, while providing remarkable flexibility and scalability for enterprise-level operations, also represents one of the most critical under-protected attack surfaces existing today. This concurrently introduces significant vulnerabilities that can be exploited by malicious actors. 

In this session, Austin Gadient, CTO and Co-Founder of Vali Cyber, will walk through real-world attack vectors recently leveraged against VMware ESXi and similar virtualization platforms, including remote code execution, Active Directory misconfigurations, and credential-based intrusions. Drawing on lessons from incidents like the MGM and MITRE breaches, Austin will dissect how attackers gain footholds, pivot across virtual environments, and execute hypervisor-level ransomware. The session will conclude with actionable best practices for security leaders looking to harden their virtual infrastructure. 

This talk is designed for technical audiences concerned with defending critical virtualization layers and aims to bridge the gap between vulnerability awareness and operational resilience.

session.title: Tinker Tailor LLM Spy: Investigate & Respond to Attacks on GenAI Chatbots
session.description:
It’s coming, and you aren’t ready—your first generative AI chatbot incident. GenAI chatbots, leveraging LLMs, are revolutionizing customer engagement by providing real-time, automated 24/7 chat support. But when your company’s virtual agent starts responding inappropriately to requests and handing out customer PII to anyone that asks nicely, who are they going to call? You.

You’ve seen the cool prompt injection attack demos and may even be vaguely aware of preventions like LLM guardrails; but are you ready to investigate and respond when those preventions inevitably fail? Would you even know where to start? It’s time to connect traditional investigation and response procedures with the exciting new world of GenAI chatbots.

In this talk, you’ll learn how to investigate and respond to the unique threats targeting these systems. You’ll discover new methods for isolating attacks, gathering information, and getting to the root cause of an incident using AI defense tooling and LLM guardrails. You’ll come away from this talk with a playbook for investigating and responding to this new class of GenAI incidents and the preparation steps you’ll need to take before your company’s chatbot responses start going viral—for the wrong reasons.

session.title: Cloud’s Dirty Little Secret: It Was Misconfigs All Along
session.description:
Everyone loves to chase the next big exploit, but here’s the truth: most cloud breaches don’t start with some elite APT magic — they start with someone leaving the front door wide open. Misconfigurations are the silent killer of cloud security, and they’re everywhere.

After tearing down hundreds of Azure deployments across industries, I’ve seen the same screw-ups again and again — from Fortune 500s to scrappy startups. In this talk, I’ll walk through the most common cloud security faceplants, why they’re so easy to make (and miss), and how to actually fix them before someone else finds them first.

If you’ve ever wondered what cloud security looks like when it fails quietly, this is your wake-up call.

session.title: The Good Business: How to Bootstrap a Business to $10M and Beyond
session.description:
This talk is a transparent account of my attempt to build a “good business”—one that is both financially successful and deeply ethical. It’s a transparent account of our journey from starting with nothing to achieving over $10M in revenue, and it’s a guide for anyone seeking to create a business that stands for integrity, craftsmanship, and meaningful impact. By reflecting on our story, sharing hard-earned lessons, and offering a roadmap. 

I hope the audience will gain inspiration, clarity, and confidence to embark on their own entrepreneurial journey. They’ll find comfort in knowing that the road to success is long and challenging—but entirely possible. With a roadmap to avoid common pitfalls and the motivation to persevere, readers will feel equipped to build their own “good business,” grounded in purpose, integrity, and sustainable success.

session.title: Exploit Intelligence with Agentic AI: Patch What Matters
session.description:
If you’re tired of dashboards that label everything “critical,” come see how exploit-aware AI puts scarce remediation hours where they count.

Security teams are drowning in vulnerability data while attackers automate exploit development and weaponization. Fixing everything is impossible; fixing what truly matters demands context: understanding not just CVSS math but whether a working exploit is circulating now, how that code path is used in your stack, and what blast-radius it creates for the business.

This talk shows how AI-native multi-agent systems can mine, reason over, and actionably surface exploit intelligence at machine speed. We trace the journey of how autonomous agents crawl exploit databases, KEVs, GitHub PoCs and correlate artefacts with call-graph and application context, providing a single enriched “fix-first” list your team can stomach.

Attendees will learn:
• How scoping of exploit maturity and organizational context can be automated with existing tools;
• Design patterns for trustworthy agentic workflows (AI scaffolding, human-in-the-loop)
• Metrics that prove noise reduction and dramatic days-to-patch gains.

session.title: BSidesNYC 0x05 Closing Ceremonies
